description: User input used in SQL string formatting without parameterization
secure_example: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,))
remediation_steps: Use parameterized queries
Use ORM query builder
Validate input type

description: User-controlled input interpolated into raw SQL query via Sequelize or direct database driver
secure_example: sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?', { replacements: [req.body.email], type: QueryTypes.SELECT })
remediation_steps: Use parameterized replacements in Sequelize
Use Sequelize model finders instead of raw queries
Validate and sanitize user input before query construction

description: User-controlled data written to DOM via innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write without sanitization
secure_example: document.getElementById('output').textContent = userInput;
remediation_steps: Use textContent instead of innerHTML
Sanitize HTML with DOMPurify before insertion
Use Content Security Policy headers

description: User input rendered in Flask response without escaping or passed through Markup() or |safe filter
secure_example: return render_template('welcome.html', name=request.args.get('name'))
remediation_steps: Use Jinja2 templates with auto-escaping enabled
Never mark user input as safe with Markup() or |safe
Sanitize user input with bleach before rendering

description: User input passed to subprocess with shell=True, allowing arbitrary command execution
secure_example: subprocess.run(['grep', user_input, '/var/log/app.log'], shell=False)
remediation_steps: Use subprocess with shell=False and pass arguments as a list
Use shlex.quote() if shell=True is absolutely required
Validate user input against an allowlist of expected values

description: User input from HTTP request passed directly to child_process exec, enabling command injection
secure_example: const { execFile } = require('child_process'); execFile('ping', ['-c', '4', sanitizedHost]);
remediation_steps: Use execFile or spawn instead of exec to avoid shell interpretation
Validate input against allowlists
Use a dedicated library for the specific task instead of shelling out

description: User-supplied filename or path used to open or read files without validation, allowing directory traversal
vulnerable_example: with open(os.path.join('/uploads', request.args['filename'])) as f: return f.read()
secure_example: safe_path = pathlib.Path('/uploads').resolve() / filename; if not str(safe_path.resolve()).startswith(str(pathlib.Path('/uploads').resolve())): abort(403)
remediation_steps: Resolve the full path and verify it stays within the intended base directory
Use os.path.basename() to strip directory components from user input
Use an allowlist of permitted filenames

description: User-supplied path parameter used to read files from the server without proper sanitization
secure_example: const safeName = path.basename(req.params.filename); const filePath = path.join(__dirname, 'uploads', safeName); if (!filePath.startsWith(path.join(__dirname, 'uploads'))) return res.status(403).end();
remediation_steps: Use path.basename() to strip directory traversal sequences
Resolve the path and verify it remains under the intended root
Use express.static() with proper configuration for serving static files

description: User input concatenated into LDAP filter string without escaping special characters
secure_example: from ldap3.utils.conv import escape_filter_chars; safe_user = escape_filter_chars(username); conn.search('dc=example,dc=com', f'(uid={safe_user})')
remediation_steps: Escape special LDAP characters using ldap3.utils.conv.escape_filter_chars
Validate user input against expected patterns (alphanumeric only)
Use parameterized LDAP queries where supported

cwe_name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
description: Use of weak or deprecated cryptographic algorithms such as DES, RC4, or Blowfish for encryption
vulnerable_example: from Crypto.Cipher import DES; cipher = DES.new(key, DES.MODE_ECB)
secure_example: from cryptography.fernet import Fernet; key = Fernet.generate_key(); cipher = Fernet(key)
remediation_steps: Use AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 for symmetric encryption
Use cryptography.fernet for simple symmetric encryption needs
Avoid ECB mode; use authenticated encryption modes (GCM, CCM)

description: Use of MD5 or SHA1 for password hashing or security-sensitive operations
secure_example: import bcrypt; password_hash = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt())
remediation_steps: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 for password hashing
Use SHA-256 or SHA-3 for non-password hashing needs
Use hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac with high iteration count if bcrypt is unavailable

description: Passwords, API keys, or secrets hardcoded directly in source code
secure_example: DB_PASSWORD = os.environ.get('DB_PASSWORD'); conn = psycopg2.connect(host=os.environ['DB_HOST'], password=DB_PASSWORD)
remediation_steps: Store secrets in environment variables
Use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
Use .env files that are excluded from version control via .gitignore

description: API keys, passwords, or tokens hardcoded in JavaScript source files
vulnerable_example: const client = new S3Client({ credentials: { accessKeyId: 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE', secretAccessKey: 'wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY' }});
secure_example: const client = new S3Client({ credentials: fromEnv() });
remediation_steps: Load credentials from environment variables using process.env
Use AWS SDK credential providers (fromEnv, fromIni, fromSSO)
Store secrets in a .env file excluded from version control

description: Deserializing untrusted data with pickle, which can execute arbitrary code during unpickling
secure_example: data = json.loads(request.data)
remediation_steps: Use JSON or MessagePack for serialization of untrusted data
If pickle is required, use hmac to verify data integrity before deserializing
Use yaml.safe_load() instead of yaml.load() for YAML parsing

description: Django view handling sensitive operations without authentication decorators or permission checks
remediation_steps: Add @login_required decorator to all views that modify data
Use @permission_required for role-based access control
Implement Django REST framework permission classes for API endpoints

description: User input used in sensitive operations without type checking, length limits, or format validation
remediation_steps: Validate input types and ranges before use
Use schema validation libraries like marshmallow or pydantic
Set reasonable bounds on numeric inputs and string lengths

cwe_name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: Detailed error messages, stack traces, or internal system information exposed to end users
vulnerable_example:
except Exception as e:
    return JsonResponse({'error': traceback.format_exc()}, status=500)
remediation_steps: Log detailed errors server-side, return generic messages to clients
Set DEBUG=False in production Django settings
Implement custom error handlers that hide implementation details

description: User-supplied URL passed directly to HTTP request library, enabling access to internal services
vulnerable_example: url = request.args.get('url'); response = requests.get(url); return response.text
secure_example:
url = request.args.get('url')
parsed = urllib.parse.urlparse(url)
if parsed.hostname in ALLOWED_HOSTS and parsed.scheme in ('http', 'https'):
    response = requests.get(url, allow_redirects=False)
remediation_steps: Validate URLs against an allowlist of permitted domains
Block requests to private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x)
Disable HTTP redirects or re-validate after following redirects

cwe_name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
description: XML parser configured to process external entities, enabling file disclosure and SSRF attacks
vulnerable_example: from lxml import etree; doc = etree.parse(request.files['xml'])
secure_example: from defusedxml import ElementTree; doc = ElementTree.parse(request.files['xml'])
remediation_steps: Use the defusedxml library instead of standard library XML parsers
Disable external entity processing: parser.setFeature(handler.feature_external_ges, False)
If using lxml, set resolve_entities=False and no_network=True in the parser

cwe_name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)
description: User-supplied URL used directly in HTTP redirect without validation, enabling phishing attacks
remediation_steps: Validate that redirect URLs are relative paths starting with /
Use an allowlist of permitted redirect domains
Use url_for() to generate internal URLs instead of accepting user input

cwe_name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)
description: User input from query parameter used directly in res.redirect without URL validation
remediation_steps: Validate redirect targets are relative paths
Maintain an allowlist of valid redirect destinations
Use URL parsing to verify the hostname matches your domain

description: Use of the random module for security-sensitive operations like token generation or password reset
secure_example: token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
remediation_steps: Use the secrets module for security-sensitive random values
Use os.urandom() for cryptographic random bytes
Use secrets.token_hex() or secrets.token_urlsafe() for token generation

description: Django view decorated with @csrf_exempt or CSRF middleware disabled, removing CSRF protection
remediation_steps: Remove @csrf_exempt decorators from state-changing views
Ensure CsrfViewMiddleware is in MIDDLEWARE settings
Include {% csrf_token %} in all POST forms

cwe_name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers
description: User input injected into HTTP response headers without stripping CRLF characters, allowing header injection
vulnerable_example:
response = make_response('OK')
response.headers['X-Custom'] = request.args.get('value')
return response
secure_example:
value = request.args.get('value', '').replace('\r', '').replace('\n', '')
response = make_response('OK')
response.headers['X-Custom'] = value
return response
remediation_steps: Strip \r and \n characters from any user input used in headers
Use framework-provided header setting methods that auto-sanitize
Validate header values against expected formats

cwe_name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)
description: Django view using user-supplied input to construct redirect URL via string formatting instead of reverse()
remediation_steps: Use django.utils.http.url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme to validate redirect targets
Use django.urls.reverse() to generate URLs from view names
Default to a safe redirect target when validation fails

cwe_name: Concurrent Execution Using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization
description: Time-of-check to time-of-use race condition where file existence or permissions are checked before use
remediation_steps: Use EAFP (try/except) instead of LBYL (check-then-act) for file operations
Use file locking (fcntl.flock) for concurrent access scenarios
Use atomic file operations (os.rename for atomic replace)

cwe_name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: Flask application running with debug mode enabled, exposing interactive debugger and detailed tracebacks
secure_example:
app = Flask(__name__)
app.run(host='0.0.0.0', debug=os.environ.get('FLASK_DEBUG', 'false').lower() == 'true')
remediation_steps: Never hardcode debug=True; use environment variables
Use a WSGI server like gunicorn in production instead of app.run()
Ensure DEBUG is set to False in production configuration

cwe_name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code
description: User-controlled input passed to eval() or exec(), allowing arbitrary code execution
secure_example: import ast; result = ast.literal_eval(request.args.get('expression'))
remediation_steps: Use ast.literal_eval() for safe evaluation of literal expressions
Implement a safe expression parser for mathematical operations
Never pass user input to eval(), exec(), or compile()

cwe_name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
description: Django model created or updated directly from request data without field filtering, allowing mass assignment
remediation_steps: Use Django forms or serializers to validate and filter input fields
Explicitly list allowed fields when creating or updating objects
Never pass request data directly to model constructors with **kwargs

cwe_name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
description: JWT token decoded without specifying allowed algorithms, potentially accepting 'none' algorithm
secure_example: payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=['HS256'])
remediation_steps: Always specify explicit algorithms list excluding 'none'
Use asymmetric algorithms (RS256) for distributed systems
Set verify=True and verify_exp=True (defaults in PyJWT >= 2.0)

description: Use of insecure temporary file creation methods that are vulnerable to symlink attacks
secure_example:
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', delete=False) as f:
    f.write(data)
    tmp_path = f.name
remediation_steps: Use tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile or tempfile.mkstemp for secure temp file creation
Use tempfile.TemporaryDirectory for temporary directories
Never construct temp file paths manually using /tmp/

cwe_name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic
description: User input passed directly to MongoDB query operators without sanitization, enabling NoSQL injection
vulnerable_example: const user = await User.findOne({ username: req.body.username, password: req.body.password });
remediation_steps: Cast user input to expected types using String(), Number(), etc.
Use mongo-sanitize to strip $ operators from user input
Never compare passwords directly in queries; use bcrypt comparison

description: User input from request reflected directly in HTTP response body without encoding
secure_example:
const escapeHtml = require('escape-html'); app.get('/search', (req, res) => { res.send(`

Results for: ${escapeHtml(req.query.q)}

`); });
remediation_steps: Use a templating engine with auto-escaping (EJS, Handlebars)
HTML-encode user input before including in responses
Set Content-Security-Policy headers to mitigate impact

description: HTTPS request made with SSL certificate verification disabled, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks
secure_example: response = requests.get('https://api.example.com/data', verify='/path/to/ca-bundle.crt')
remediation_steps: Always use verify=True (the default) for requests
If using a custom CA, point verify to the CA bundle path
Never suppress InsecureRequestWarning in production code

description: Regular expression with catastrophic backtracking potential applied to user-controlled input
secure_example: pattern = re.compile(r'^a+$'); match = pattern.match(user_input[:1000])
remediation_steps: Avoid nested quantifiers in regular expressions
Set a timeout on regex operations using the regex library's timeout parameter
Limit the length of input strings before applying regex

cwe_name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes
description: User-controlled input used to set arbitrary object properties, enabling prototype pollution attacks
remediation_steps: Reject keys like __proto__, constructor, and prototype during object merging
Use Object.create(null) for dictionary-like objects
Use Map instead of plain objects for user-controlled key-value storage
